NAME: Haynes Early Education Center
Program Description:: As minority business women dedicated to the work we do, our extensive experience in education and your deep roots in the community, especially through your work with Boston Public Schools, speak volumes about your commitment to creating opportunities for young people. By establishing a pipeline to higher artistic schools, you've helped pave the way for many students to pursue advanced education in the arts, furthering their creative talents and academic achievements. This dedication to empowering the next generation through education and the arts is truly inspiring.

NAME: Albert D Holland School of Technology
School Name:: Albert D. Holland Highschool of Technology
Program Description:: Located inside a Boston Public School, an 826 Boston’s Writers’ Room provides classroom support for writing assignments throughout the school day, in addition to holding after-school activities and clubs ranging from a slam poetry team to a student-run literary journal. Operating as a fully-staffed writing center based on the university model, 826 Boston staff and trained volunteers work directly with students to improve writing skills while at the same time practicing self-expression in a safe, creative space. The Writers' Room is also a hub for publishing, and students participating in publishing projects feel the pride of authorship that comes wiht publication.
Explanation of Student Fees: There are no student fees, but we do ask for a commitment of $10K a year from school partners toward the full cost of $125K annually that it takes to operate a Writers' Room. 826 Boston fundraises the difference.

NAME: Henderson K-12 Inclusion School Lower
Program Description::
Abilities Dance Boston’s (ADB) Community Engagement Program brings fun, inclusive Adaptive Dance classes to schools to show that anyone can be a dancer!

In our classes, students start with a gentle warmup that includes breathing and a short check-in to connect mind and body. We move through a seated or standing ballet-inspired section with dance vocabulary, explore creative movement for the upper and lower body, and build short group dances and stories together. Students also get to see a performance by ADB’s professional company and share their thoughts in a class discussion.

Our classes focus on each student’s growth, creativity, and joy—not on perfect steps. Together, we build strength, flexibility, coordination, confidence, imagination, and teamwork skills—all while having fun!

NAME: Mather Elementary School
Program Description:: "Dance with Books" is a Dance Education program for students in Grades K thru 2nd grade.  Dance books are used as a springboard for students to create and explore movements, learn dances from different countries, and practice movement skills:  balance, coordination, and control.  "Dance with Books" lessons were written by Sharon Shakur, MA, an expert in the field of Dance Education. "Dance with Books" lessons promote performing arts literacy: speaking, listening, and reading about Dance.
Explanation of Student Fees: Parents pay $10.oo for each "Dance with Books" class that Ms. Shakur teaches.  These fees are waived for Parents who are unable to pay due to financial hardship conditions.

NAME: TechBoston Academy
Program Description::
Our apprenticeship program offers 8th grade students exposure to the culture of work through apprenticeships. As part of their school day, students at our partner schools receive 24 hours of programming over 12 weeks, which includes 6 weekly one-hour preparatory classes and 12-18 hours of a workplace-based apprenticeship. Preparatory classes help students identify their strengths and build a toolkit of communication and self-presentation techniques. Based on their interests, students are matched with a business partner where they spend 2-3 hours a week for six weeks. Students work alongside a mentor who assigns entry-level work tasks, supervises their work, and offers insight into his/her field of business and related careers. AL staff works with our business partners to create experiences that are developmentally appropriate and engaging for the students. To date, 306 students have completed apprenticeships.

Apprentices gain valuable skills, as well as the sense of ‘belonging’ to a professional community. Students apprentice individually or in small groups. AL staff visit each site during the apprenticeship to check in with employers and to observe students at work. As a result of participating in Apprentice Learning, students acquire knowledge and learn:
• Appropriate workplace behavior, language, dress, and mannerisms;
• Presentation and oral communication skills, and the ability to advocate for oneself;
• How to engage confidently with unfamiliar adults in unfamiliar settings; and
• How attendance, punctuality and persistence are essential to academic and career success. 

Our partner schools have included the apprenticeship program as part of students’ eighth grade graduation portfolios. Every student at our partner schools graduates with a resume in hand and intensive support in finding a summer job or enrichment program.

Participating organizations include engineering labs, hospitals, retail stores and non-profits. A partial list of worksite partners includes: Related Beal, Stantec, Boston Society of Architects, Loop Lab, Netsuite Oracle, Community Boatbuilding of Boston and others.
Explanation of Student Fees: There are no fees for students to participate.

NAME: Condon K-8 School
Program Description::
We believe children are experts at getting their needs met.  Often this requires children to engage in maladaptive behaviors, which we view as their way of communicating their needs.  We utilize non-directive child-centered therapy to empower children to express their thoughts, feelings, and behaviors.  
Younger children often use play- their most natural form of learning and communication- to re-work problems, understand and identify feelings, become sensitive to the process of coping, experience a feeling of greater sense of control and to safely grow and learn emotionally, becoming trusting of self and assuming greater self-responsibility. 
With these same objectives we engage older children utilizing a person-centered therapy approach.  Adolescents and young people are naturally in a phase of life of self-discovery and meaning making.  Providing unconditional care and allowing them to lead the therapeutic process supports their emotional, social, and cognitive development.

NAME: Albert D Holland School of Technology
School Name:: Albert D. Holland Highschool of Technology
Program Description::
Building on over 30 years of engaging young people in the creative process during out-of-school time, Artists For Humanity (AFH) is now collaborating with schools on our daytime school partnerships initiative, AFH Co-Lab. 

AFH Co-Lab shares the AFH Model and creative studio access with schools during the day, facilitating collaborations with teachers on their work to promote advanced skills development in visual art, industrial design, digital media and experiential learning in STEM+Arts (STEAM), all within AFH’s context of community engagement, youth leadership and building equity.

In AFH's core program the Youth Arts Enterprise, AFH teen artists and designers work collaboratively with AFH mentors to find creative solutions to their client's needs. In AFH Co-Lab, teen participants take on the role of AFH teen artists and designers, and their clients are their school and their community. 

Youth collaborate with AFH mentors on projects in Painting (Fine Art and Murals), Creative Technology, Graphic Design, Photography, Video Production, Animation and 3D Design (3D Installations and Furniture). Teachers, staff or community members select a design project that best meets their final outcome and educational objectives. Participants are guided through the design process with AFH mentors and can complete the entire process or work on select steps with AFH staff; the length of each project is informed by the amount of time that schools can dedicate to the collaboration.
Program Areas:: 'Academic Support & Enrichment','Arts Programming','College, Career & Life Readiness'
Program Subareas:: 'Career Advising','Job & Employment Preparation','Multidisciplinary','Social-Emotional Learning','Student Leadership Development','Visual Arts','Work-based Learning'
Explanation of Student Fees: School partnership fees are priced per project. Pricing is based on mentor time, prep time, fabrication and material costs. AFH Co-Lab also supports school partnerships through public and private grants.

NAME: Ellis Elementary School
Program Description:: The World Music/Percussion residency with teaching artist Júlio Santos will explore Latin drumming as well as the drums and rhythms from other places in the world including the Middle East, Africa and the USA. Since all drumming and rhythm are related to each other, students will explore a mixture of instruments and rhythms for creative and fun musical experiments! World Music/Percussion includes storytelling and cultural learning which will help students to understand better how to work in groups while respecting each other’s individualities within a culturally responsive approach.
Time: School Day (Class Time), School Day (Non-Class Time)
Explanation of Student Fees: No student fees.

NAME: Mario Umana Academy
Program Description:: Bilingual Latino instructor(s) will offer classes in traditional and contemporary Latin dance, providing an opportunity for artistic expression, a means to connect with an important cultural heritage, a chance to exercise and develop social skills, and a platform for demonstrating pride in one’s community.

NAME: Dever Elementary School
Program Description::
BalletRox provides weekly instructional dance classes to BPS students as structured, integrated part of their school day. We work closely with partner schools to provide the most appropriate inclusive and accessible dance programs for all students, and adapt virtual classes to meet the needs of each school, classroom and students. Additionally, BalletRox commits to provide at least two fully funded dance scholarships per school, to attend BalletRox Dance!, our after-school community dance program located in Jamaica Plain.

The BalletRox approach to in-school programming is based on the premise that all children benefit from integrated arts instruction as part of the school day and dance is a particularly effective means of artistic exploration for most children, including those with special needs and English Language Learners. In addition to learning dance skills, participation in dance offers students opportunity to develop and practice important life-long skills – skills such as collaboration, communication, creativity and innovation, and critical thinking that are necessary for success and lead to greater sense of self-efficacy.  Students utilize these skills during weekly dance classes and the creation and performance of a collaborative dance and culminating recital.

NAME: Blackstone Elementary School
Program Description::
Since the fall of 2009, the Boch Center has partnered with Boston Public Schools in the "In-School Residency Program," (formally Target Arts) an artist residency and ticket access program generously funded by private donors and the Boch Center.  

Students in participating schools develop original works of art in various mediums centered on themes determined in collaboration with a classroom teacher. The residency also incorporates multiple selected children’s books that helps accentuate the theme, with each student receiving a copy of one of the books. 
The residencies are structured over the course of 10 weeks, with teaching artists visiting classrooms twice per week for one hour.  
At a time when arts dollars are being cut from school budgets, the Boch Center strives to fill the gap in arts education and play a leadership role in arts program delivery and resource coordination.
Explanation of Student Fees: There are no student fees for this program.

NAME: Grew Elementary School
Program Name:: Boston Childrens Chorus In School Program
Program Description:: . For the past 20 years, BCC has been bringing children ages 7–18 together across differences of race, religion, and socioeconomic status in a celebration of shared humanity and love of singing. Among numerous awards, BCC was honored to receive the National Arts and Humanities Youth Program Award in 2013. More recent highlights include performances at the Welcome to Earthshot Boston 2022 Ceremony, at the Boston Celtics 2022 home opener to honor basketball legend and civil rights advocate Bill Russell at TD Garden, and our annual Martin Luther King, Jr. concert at Symphony Hall in January, 2023, featuring more than 300 youth singers and guest artists and speakers such as Boston mayor Michelle Wu, Embrace Boston’s Imari Paris Jenkins, and our own Hubie Jones. Boston Children’s Chorus (BCC) was founded in 2003 by activist and social worker Hubie Jones to connect Greater Boston communities through choral music. For the past 20 years, BCC has been bringing children ages 7–18 together across differences of race, religion, and socioeconomic status in a celebration of shared humanity and love of singing. Our curriculum framework empowers singers to achieve proficiency in: personal competency; artistic competency; professional competency and civic competency.

NAME: Boston International High School & Newcomers Academy
Program Description::
The Boston Children’s Hospital Neighborhood Partnerships program (BCHNP) is a school-based behavioral health program committed to working with school communities most impacted by systemic inequities. BCHNP is a part of the Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences at Boston Children’s Hospital. Our purpose is to promote the social, emotional, and behavioral health and well-being of students, caregivers, and staff in partnership with the Boston Public Schools (BPS). We collaborate with community members and organizations to provide clinical care, consultation, professional development, research, and advocacy. 

The Training and Access Project is a professional development project for school communities on
social-emotional learning and behavioral health.  TAP's live webinar series is tailored for social workers, psychologists, school counselors, nurses, and other school-based clinicians working in Boston Public Schools. Each topic includes key concepts, tools, and strategies. Additional resources and CEUs for social workers, psychologists, nurses, and LMHCs are available.  Click here register for upcoming webinars and information on accessing past webinar recordings and resources.
https://tapbostonforschools.org/clinicians.html

NAME: Higginson/Lewis K-8 School
Program Description::
The Resolved Program uses the core elements of policy debate, mentorship, and affinity community building to engage and empower Black and Latino boys and young men in middle and high school. Through the program, cohorts of ten to fifteen Black and Latino young men from each partner school meet twice a week to have meaningful conversations, explore their identities, reflect on their future plans, and use the skills of debate to evaluate and form arguments or advocacy about issues in their communities. 

In the weekly sessions, we take a culturally responsive approach that privileges the voices of the students, giving them agency and choice in the topics explored. We also bring in texts and other relevant materials to inform the conversation—such as providing a different viewpoint. The sessions are led by two BDL alumni who are Black and/or Latino men. We will also engage leaders in the community who are men of color to speak with our students. These community leaders will not only further the students’  knowledge of the subjects but also serve as potential role models.

Participants of the program will learn about the tools of argumentation, such as evaluating evidence, questioning claims, and conducting research. They will also learn life skills: managing conflict, public speaking, and self-advocacy. In addition, they will have the opportunity to reflect on future plans and learn how to set SMART goals to achieve them.

NAME: Lyon Elementary School
Program Description::
The After-School Debate League operates English-language teams and Spanish-language teams in middle schools and high schools within area public schools. Debate team practices are held on-site at member schools and led by teachers who serve as coaches. In some cases, BDL alumni also serve as lead or assistant coaches. Debate season spans October through March each school year, and students attend up to 8 tournaments throughout the season where they compete against other schools in the Novice, JV, or Varsity divisions.

For students participating in our Debate en Espanol programming, the structure of the experience is exactly the same and takes place entirely in Spanish – materials are in Spanish, coaching takes place in Spanish, debates take place in Spanish, and decisions and feedback are shared in written and verbal form in Spanish.
Explanation of Student Fees: There are no student fees.

Debate season spans October through March each school year, and students attend up to 8 tournaments throughout the season where they compete against other schools in the Novice, JV, or Varsity divisions.

For students participating in our Debate en Espanol programming, the structure of the experience is exactly the same and takes place entirely in Spanish – materials are in Spanish, coaching takes place in Spanish, debates take place in Spanish, and decisions and feedback are shared in written and verbal form in Spanish.
Explanation of Student Fees: There are no student fees.

NAME: Albert D Holland School of Technology
School Name:: Albert D. Holland Highschool of Technology
Program Description::
Boston HERC's Passport to College Program is a Latino-led, culturally-sensitive, college readiness program focused on failure-proofing first-generation students of color throughout their journey through college, enabling them to break the cycle of poverty and become leaders in their community. We believe that closing the Opportunity and Achievement Gaps is possible – and if there is any community poised to do this it is Boston. Doing so will require "re-wiring" the minds of BPS first-generation students and preparing them to lead in the competitive economy that is already shaping Boston’s future. Already, there are Boston HERC Passport Coaches serving BPS first-generation students in 9 non-exam high schools, imbuing these youth with what we call the Habits of the Mind: 21st Century skills that are indispensable for their success in college and in the innovation-driven Boston workplace they will soon be inhabiting. Currently fewer than 30% of Latino (29.7%) and black students (28.9%) graduating from BPS are completing college [Boston Foundation, 2016]. In stark contrast to BPS's college completion trends, 87% of the first-generation students who go on to college from Passport since the inception of the program in 2004 have graduated within 5-years of enrollment.

Each school's headmasters, teachers, and counselors identify Passport cohorts in each grade selecting students who are First-Generation, low-income, and have between a 2.0 and 3.0 GPA. In addition to tutoring, financial aid assistance, parent workshops and college tours, the core of the Passport experience is devoted to providing repeated exposure to the Habits of the Mind – (1) the development of effective project management skills and study habits; (2) time management skills; (3) financial management; and, (4) relationship management and conflict resolution skills - which are especially scarce among first-generation students. Passport Coaches (who are all Black or Latina, and are all first-generation themselves) spend an average of 15 to 20 hours per week, throughout the school year, providing their Passport Scholars no less than 1.5 hours to 2 hours of instruction each week in a classroom cohort setting, in addition to out-of-class individual counseling.Beyond high school, into college, in addition to follow-up by the Passport Director of Alumni Success, 50 first-generation Passport Scholars each year, for two years, enrolling at three target institutions of higher education will be paired with a Success Intern from our corporate and non-profit partners, dramatically increasing the likelihood they will complete college.
Program Subareas:: 'College Advising','College Applications','College Visits','Financial Aid','Financial Aid Advising/FAFSA Completion','Identity Exploration','Personal Financial Literacy/Management','Post Secondary Planning','Study Skills Development'

Passport Coaches (who are all Black or Latina, and are all first-generation themselves) spend an average of 15 to 20 hours per week, throughout the school year, providing their Passport Scholars no less than 1.5 hours to 2 hours of instruction each week in a classroom cohort setting, in addition to out-of-class individual counseling.Beyond high school, into college, in addition to follow-up by the Passport Director of Alumni Success, 50 first-generation Passport Scholars each year, for two years, enrolling at three target institutions of higher education will be paired with a Success Intern from our corporate and non-profit partners, dramatically increasing the likelihood they will complete college.

NAME: Perry Elementary School
Program Description::
Boston Music Project is a creative youth development program committed to ensuring the long-term social-emotional and musical success of Boston youth. Through before, after, and in-school programs, students (K-12) take part in programming that includes 1) instrumental instruction (strings, brass, winds), 2) general music, and/or 3) digital music.

The Boston Music Project is inspired by El Sistema, the Venezuelan initiative that uses music education as a vehicle for social change. The El Sistema philosophy of having students help each other through the music-learning process is at the heart of our mission, and as a result, our program creates not only great musicians but also caring individuals who will grow up to be active members of society. We believe that every child can learn to express music deeply, receive its many benefits, and make different critical life choices as a result of this learning.
Explanation of Student Fees: BMP Before/After school programming tuition is $45/week for 6.5 hours of weekly small-group and private instruction. We offer sliding-scale financial assistance to all students, who pay anything from $0-39/week. We never turn a child away for financial (or any other) reasons.

NAME: Mildred Avenue K-8 School
Program Description::
Motivate is available to any PreK-2 teacher who feels their classroom would benefit from extra academic or socio-emotional support. The focus of these matches is in the subject areas of reading, writing, math and science. Motivate academic mentors meet with the class for at least one-hour each week over the school year during academic subject blocks. Many of our volunteers spend more time each week with their classrooms.

Motivate is an effective way to support PreK-2nd grade students who lack the attention span to focus for a whole hour with a mentor. It allows the entire classroom to benefit from additional adult support, permitting students to self-select for help when they need it. 

Mentors support students during centers, independent work time, or when the teacher is pulling small groups. They may walk around and support different students as needed, or facilitate a small group.

Motivate academic mentors meet with the class for at least one-hour each week over the school year during academic subject blocks. Many of our volunteers spend more time each week with their classrooms.

Motivate is an effective way to support PreK-2nd grade students who lack the attention span to focus for a whole hour with a mentor. It allows the entire classroom to benefit from additional adult support, permitting students to self-select for help when they need it. 

Mentors support students during centers, independent work time, or when the teacher is pulling small groups. They may walk around and support different students as needed, or facilitate a small group.

NAME: Community Academy
Program Description::
.In the Independent Learning Support model, mentors are connected with Boston Public Schools students who have self-identified or been identified by their families as benefiting from extra, focused academic support. Once matched, mentors will meet with students on Google Meet for a minimum of one hour per week for the duration of the school year.

In this model, mentoring sessions are not bound by school hours – students and mentors can meet at an agreed-upon time to review classwork, work on projects, and build skills in the student’s area of need.

NAME: Hennigan Elementary School
Program Description::
Boston Scores offers 4-6th grade students the chance to join a team at their school to learn the "beautiful game" of soccer, compete in game days against other schools and build up their nutritional literacy. 

Students remain with their teams for the academic enrichment portion of our program. During the 8-week fall season, students develop their writing and self-expression skills while preparing to compete in a team-based Poetry Slam.

During the 10-week spring season, students work with their Coach-Mentors to develop a Service Learning project to identify and address a community need. Students complete in an end-of-season festival ("Jamboree") where they compete in a friendly soccer tournament against other Elementary Schools and present their Service Learning Project results.

Each participating school will have both Boys and Girls teams. There is room for 15 players on each team. Scores works with each school partner to identify and recruit internal school personnel to serve as our Coach-Mentors. Scores provides multiple training sessions for Coach-Mentors throughout the school year and also pays an hourly wage to Site Coordinators & Coach-Mentors.

Program hours are flexible based on Extended Learning Time and other local factors. Typical programs meet 4-5 days per week.
Explanation of Student Fees: Our program is free to students

NAME: Warren-Prescott K-8 School
Program Description::
Boston Scores offers grade 7th -8th students the chance to join a team at their school to learn the "beautiful game" of soccer and compete in games against other schools. Student simultaneously developing their financial literacy through team-based service projects using our One Hen social entrepreneurship curriculum.
Students practice soccer twice per week using the Soccer For Success curriculum developed by the United States Soccer Foundation. Students compete in games against other schools once per week.
Students remain with their teams for the academic enrichment portion of our program. During the 8-week fall season, students learn basic concepts of financial literacy such as how to calculate revenues, expenses & profits, and to understand loans and interest rates. They identify a social issue that is relevant to their communities and develop a business plan for a product or service that will drive awareness of the issue and generate profits that can be donated to address it.  

The fall season culminates with "The Pitch" in November. At this special event all partner Middle Schools present their One Hen business plans to a panel of business leaders. Student teams receive micro-loans of varying sizes to fund their ideas based upon the strength of the presentation.
During the 10-week spring season, students will return to their soccer teams for two practices per week and one inter-scholastic game day.
In the classroom, students will use the spring season to bring to life the One Hen business plan developed during the fall. Armed with a micro-loan, the team will now create their product or service, market it to their target audience and have "Sell Days" to generate profits. They will re-pay their loans and then donate their profits to a designated non-profit organization chosen by the students. The spring season culminates with our "Blast" special event in June. Students compete in a soccer tournament against other partner Middle Schools and then share a presentation on their final One Hen project results.

Each participating school has gender-specific Boys and Girls teams. There is room for 15 players on each team. Scores works with each school partner to identify & recruit internal school personnel to serve as our Coach-Mentors. Scores provides training sessions for Coach-Mentors throughout the school year & also pays an hourly wage to Site Coordinators and Coach-Mentors.
Explanation of Student Fees: Our program is free to students

Students practice soccer twice per week using the Soccer For Success curriculum developed by the United States Soccer Foundation. Students compete in games against other schools once per week.
Students remain with their teams for the academic enrichment portion of our program. During the 8-week fall season, students learn basic concepts of financial literacy such as how to calculate revenues, expenses & profits, and to understand loans and interest rates. They identify a social issue that is relevant to their communities and develop a business plan for a product or service that will drive awareness of the issue and generate profits that can be donated to address it.  

The fall season culminates with "The Pitch" in November. At this special event all partner Middle Schools present their One Hen business plans to a panel of business leaders. Student teams receive micro-loans of varying sizes to fund their ideas based upon the strength of the presentation.
During the 10-week spring season, students will return to their soccer teams for two practices per week and one inter-scholastic game day.
In the classroom, students will use the spring season to bring to life the One Hen business plan developed during the fall. Armed with a micro-loan, the team will now create their product or service, market it to their target audience and have "Sell Days" to generate profits. They will re-pay their loans and then donate their profits to a designated non-profit organization chosen by the students. The spring season culminates with our "Blast" special event in June. Students compete in a soccer tournament against other partner Middle Schools and then share a presentation on their final One Hen project results.

Each participating school has gender-specific Boys and Girls teams. There is room for 15 players on each team. Scores works with each school partner to identify & recruit internal school personnel to serve as our Coach-Mentors. Scores provides training sessions for Coach-Mentors throughout the school year & also pays an hourly wage to Site Coordinators and Coach-Mentors.
Explanation of Student Fees: Our program is free to students

NAME: Baldwin Early Learning Pilot Academy
Organization Name:: Boston University Wheelock College of Education and Human Development
Program Description::
These are unpaid, semester long student teachers. Their field placements in schools are required for academic program completion.

Pre-practicum students must demonstrate proficiency in Gateway Assessments to proceed to their full practicum. This is required by DESE’s updated pre-practicum guidelines. 

Student teachers (full practicum) are assessed on the DESE Candidate Assessment of Performance (CAP). The CAP aligns with the MA teacher rubric, is intended to ensure that teacher candidates are ready to teach on day one, and focuses on seven essential elements:

NAME: East Boston High School

Program Description:: GEAR UP (Gaining Early Awareness and Readiness for Undergraduate Programs) is a federally funded program awarded to the MA Department of Higher Education (DHE) with support from American Student Assistance (ASA). The Boston University program serves low-income and first-generation middle and high school youth from East Boston High School and the Mario Umana Academy K-8.  Services are based at these schools. Program participants gain awareness and preparedness for their short- and long-term educational and career goals.  The program tracks participants to degree attainment and offers transition support to first-year postsecondary enrollees.

NAME: Quincy Elementary School
Organization Name:: Boston University Wheelock College of Education and Human Development
Program Name:: BUILD (Boston University Initiative for Literacy Development
Program Description:: Established in 1997, the Boston University Initiative for Literacy Development (BUILD) is funded through Federal Work-Study (America Reads and America Counts) and Boston University.  The program recruits, trains and places Boston University undergraduates as literacy and math tutors at partner BPS K-5 schools and afterschool providers.  Tutors work both with classroom teachers (in-school tutoring) and with afterschool programs providing literacy and math tutoring.  2022-23 In-School tutoring sites include: the Hurley, Tobin, Trotter, Baldwin ELC, and the Winship.  Afterschool sites include: the Mendell, BCNC Red Oaks, Blackstone Elementary School, and the Winship.

NAME: Mattahunt Elementary School
Program Description::
Boston’s Bridge To Excellence (BBTE) is a licensed extended day educational program, serving children from K0–6th grade. Approved by the Massachusetts Department of Early Education and Care, BBTE operates inside five Boston Public Schools and two charter schools—and continues to grow. Our mission is to meet the academic and social-emotional needs of every child by providing a nurturing, engaging, and supportive learning environment before and after school.

At BBTE, we believe extended day programming should be a space for innovation, creativity, and growth. Our philosophy centers on play-based and project-based learning, fostering a love for education during times when students might otherwise be inactive. We support the development of each child and also invest in early childhood educators, ensuring a high-quality experience for both students and staff.

Our program proudly partners with community organizations including Boston University’s BUILD Program, Wheelock College, ABCD, Boston Public Library, and Boston PIC. These partnerships provide weekly literacy support through interactive activities and hands-on learning extensions. Our collaboration with Apple has brought technology into every classroom, helping students develop strong math skills in a fun, experiential way.

We also offer a unique Landscape Architecture, Engineering, and Design program for girls (Project GAP), in partnership with a local architectural college. This initiative introduces students—especially young girls—to careers in architecture, allowing them to design and build while learning about the education paths required for these fields.

Our pilot program, “Waves of Reading,” connects students with local professionals who read aloud and share their career journeys. These sessions expand literacy while inspiring students to dream big and think about their futures.
BBTE’s Extended Day Programs support student development through:
Academic enrichment and homework help
Engaging, interactive learning
Customized curriculum aligned with student needs
Specialized programs: Math, Movement, Coding, Apple Learning Apps
Architecture & design exploration
Perseverance and growth mindset training
Critical thinking and problem-solving skills
Healthy relationship-building and emotional regulation
Communication and leadership development
Teamwork and collaboration
Encouraging joy, curiosity, and autonomy in learning
At Boston’s Bridge To Excellence, we’re more than just a before-and-after school program—we’re building a bridge to brighter futures for all our children.
Time: Before School, Afterschool, Summer, School Vacation
Explanation of Student Fees:
Vouchers or Private Pay

Parents who pay private fees pay a two week fee to secure their child's slot and pay weekly to bi-weekly after that via invoice. Parents who are eligible for state vouchers follow the same process and sign a confirmation of provider form to be approved by Child Care Choices of Boston. Once approved the parent pays their voucher parent fee (if applicable) and their child secures a slot into the program and pays fees weekly for the school year, vacation weeks and Summer.

NAME: Holmes Elementary School
Program Description::
Boston’s Bridge To Excellence (BBTE) is a licensed extended day educational program, serving children from K0–6th grade. Approved by the Massachusetts Department of Early Education and Care, BBTE operates inside five Boston Public Schools and two charter schools—and continues to grow. Our mission is to meet the academic and social-emotional needs of every child by providing a nurturing, engaging, and supportive learning environment before and after school.

At BBTE, we believe extended day programming should be a space for innovation, creativity, and growth. Our philosophy centers on play-based and project-based learning, fostering a love for education during times when students might otherwise be inactive. We support the development of each child and also invest in early childhood educators, ensuring a high-quality experience for both students and staff.

Our program proudly partners with community organizations including Boston University’s BUILD Program, Wheelock College, ABCD, Boston Public Library, and Boston PIC. These partnerships provide weekly literacy support through interactive activities and hands-on learning extensions. Our collaboration with Apple has brought technology into every classroom, helping students develop strong math skills in a fun, experiential way.

We also offer a unique Landscape Architecture, Engineering, and Design program for girls (Project GAP), in partnership with a local architectural college. This initiative introduces students—especially young girls—to careers in architecture, allowing them to design and build while learning about the education paths required for these fields.

Our pilot program, “Waves of Reading,” connects students with local professionals who read aloud and share their career journeys. These sessions expand literacy while inspiring students to dream big and think about their futures.
BBTE’s Extended Day Programs support student development through:
Academic enrichment and homework help
Engaging, interactive learning
Customized curriculum aligned with student needs
Specialized programs: Math, Movement, Coding, Apple Learning Apps
Architecture & design exploration
Perseverance and growth mindset training
Critical thinking and problem-solving skills
Healthy relationship-building and emotional regulation
Communication and leadership development
Teamwork and collaboration
Encouraging joy, curiosity, and autonomy in learning
At Boston’s Bridge To Excellence, we’re more than just a before-and-after school program—we’re building a bridge to brighter futures for all our children.
Time: Before School, Afterschool, Summer, School Vacation

NAME: Brighton High School
Program Description::
The BUILD program involves three primary components:

Entrepreneurship. students enroll in a credited, in-school, Introduction to Entrepreneurship class taught by a BUILD-trained public school teacher. Students learn business fundamentals, form business teams, identify a target market, develop a marketable product, write business plans, and pitch at three showcases. Each team obtains a bank account and business license, and students sell products at school, in their communities and at BUILD-sponsored events. The year culminates with our annual pitch competitions and selling events.


Mentoring. BUILD Boston relies on 100 mentors, who annually contribute more than 8,000 volunteer hours. All mentors are college educated professionals who play a key role in helping students develop, grow their micro-enterprises and persist through high school. Our mentors form lasting relationships with students, meeting with them weekly and fostering a growth-mindset culture while helping students recognize their full potential.

Students learn the basics of entrepreneurship while improving academic skills
Students develop a business plan
Students secure seed capital through a pitch to a Venture Capitalist
Students run their own businesses
Students learn about their various postsecondary options and pursue the next step that is right for them with confidence, utilizing the resources they have available to them
Program Areas:: 'College, Career & Life Readiness','Social Emotional & Behavioral Health'
Program Subareas:: 'Career & Life Readiness','Career Speakers','College Applications','Job & Employment Preparation','Mentoring','Social Emotional Learning','Student Leadership Development','Workplace College'

NAME: Brighton High School
Organization Name:: Cathleen Stone Island Outward Bound School
Program Description:: We partner with Boston Public Schools to help close the opportunity gap by bringing students to our Harbor Island every year throughout middle school. By exposing students to unique experiences and a new environment outside their comfort zone, Cathleen Stone Island Outward Bound ignites curiosity and allows students to push past their preconceived ideas of what they can achieve. Guided by experienced instructors and supported by their teachers, students explore Cathleen Stone Island’s wide variety of ecosystems from the tide pools to the forests. During hands-on outdoor science lessons, kids explore, investigate, and develop their ideas like scientists. Small group sizes allow students to build meaningful connections with their classmates and makes it possible for instructors to provide support to students when they need it. The structure of School Year Connections is meant to extend beyond Cathleen Stone Island. During the winter months, Cathleen Stone Island Outward Bound instructors visit students in their classrooms to deliver "Crew Classes," 1-hour sessions of intentional modules focused on building trust, problem-solving and group/self-reflection

NAME: Dearborn STEM Academy
Organization Name:: Cathleen Stone Island Outward Bound School
Program Description::
In partnership with Boston Public Schools, Thompson Island Outward Bound Education Center’s Connections program aims to close the achievement gap that exists among students of diverse backgrounds. Integrating Massachusetts curriculum frameworks into hands-on field experiences, the program combines collaboration and communication skills with hands-on learning in the natural world to create opportunities for success in school, in future careers, and in the community.

Using the unique setting of Thompson Island and the Boston Harbor Islands National and State Park area as an outdoor classroom, students participate in a sequential learning initiative integrating classroom science lessons and social emotional learning with on-island experiences that progress from season to season and year to year for grades 5 through 8. They explore, discover, navigate, research, hike, and climb as they work together to overcome challenges, solve problems, and inspire each other on a path towards excellence.
Explanation of Student Fees: No student fees

NAME: Brighton High School
Program Description::
• Partnering with school leaders, we determine the best place to construct a Closet within the school building. Contractors or school facilities prepare and paint the room, install fixtures, deliver merchandise, and set up the Closet. Closets are designed to feel like small boutiques, filled with age-appropriate, fashionable items.
• School staff identify and discreetly invite students to visit the closet and “shop” for what they need. There is no limit to the number of items students can take, or the frequency with which they can visit. Students are treated with dignity and respect.
• A volunteer “Closet Champion” maintains each closet, ordering more inventory as needed. A single school maintains 1500 units or $20K in inventory (thrift value).
• We partner with other school-based programs by providing for specific clothing needs.
• For an additional charge, we can provide additional products that are not part of our standard inventory list.

NAME: Sarah Roberts Elementary School
Program Description:: Introducing our Chess Enrichment Program, catered to students in K–8th grade! This program is designed to provide a comprehensive learning experience that combines the fundamentals of chess with the development of critical thinking, problem-solving, and decision-making skills. Our team of experienced instructors is dedicated to making chess an enjoyable and enriching activity for children of all skill levels. The Chess Enrichment Program offers a structured curriculum that covers the essentials of chess for beginners while also offering opportunities for experienced players to enhance their skills. Through engaging activities and interactive lessons, students will learn to anticipate moves, formulate strategies, and think strategically. The program fosters a supportive and inclusive environment, ensuring that each child receives personalized attention and guidance from our instructors. We invite parents to enroll their children in our exciting chess enrichment program to witness the growth and development of their critical thinking abilities, problem-solving skills, and decision-making capabilities. By participating in this program, students will gain valuable skills that can be applied both on and off the chessboard. Join us today and embark on a rewarding chess journey that will not only enhance your child's cognitive abilities but also instill a lifelong love for the game of chess.

When a school partners with City Connects, a full-time City Connects Coordinator becomes embedded within the fabric of the school. The City Connects Coordinator collaborates with teachers in every classroom to review the academic, social/emotional/behavioral, physical health and family strengths and needs of every student, every year in a whole child approach. The City Connects Coordinator creates a tailored support plan for every student - leveraging a comprehensive range of prevention, intervention, and enrichment services that already exist in schools and communities. Students who are most at risk receive an in-depth review and a broader level of supports.  Using proprietary software developed for City Connects, City Connects Coordinators create a student support record for each student and constantly track services and evaluate results, making sure every student is getting what’s needed. The City Connects Coordinator works closely with students and their families, providing referrals and support that bolster the long-term health of the entire family. At City Connects schools, the City Connects Coordinator becomes the hub of student support — a single point of contact in an otherwise crowded maze of service providers. The program alleviates the burden on principals and teachers and creates an organized referral process for tailored services provided by both the school and by community-based partners.
Explanation of Student Fees: Our program is free to students

NAME: Haynes Early Education Center
Program Description::
CitySprouts’ full-time Garden Educators (GEs) lead classes one or two days a week at our 12 partner schools in Boston. The GEs facilitate sessions in the school garden classrooms for pre-kindergarten through 5th students using our Garden Science curriculum, which aligns with Massachusetts Science, Technology, and Engineering Standards. CitySprouts educators collaborate with teachers to extend and deepen students’ science knowledge and skills as well as their connection to the natural world, using the school garden as a central space for learning.

We strive to make CitySprouts lessons accessible for students of all abilities and backgrounds, to incorporate social-emotional learning and healthy foods, and to ensure that learning is hands-on and fosters curiosity and wonder.

NAME: Boston International High School & Newcomers Academy
Program Name:: Planning & Advising for Transitions to Higher Education (BU PATH)
Program Description::
BU PATH (places recent college graduates as full-time college and career advisers in 26 BPS high schools. Our highly diverse team of advisers directly represent our student population; advisers are a resource and inspiration to the young people they serve. PATH’s intervention addresses the root of the problem: lack of support for low-income students as they aspire to post-secondary options and attempt to navigate the complicated process. 
With support from PATH and school-based staff, advisers build a program that fits the needs of the school community. We actively seek to collaborate with others in order to leverage existing resources and expand opportunity. 
Advisers work intensively through one-on-one and small group meetings with upperclassmen through the entire college and career search and application process, providing the individualized and targeted advice and support. This includes planning college or career application steps, building a list of colleges or unionships in which to apply, completing admissions/financial aid applications, and enrolling at schools that are “best match/fit” to the individual’s needs. Advisers educate students about postsecondary pathways, including bachelor degrees, associate degrees, certificate programs, and career pathways, emphasizing that the path to postsecondary success does not look the same for every student. Advisers seek to work with every student, regardless of their GPA or postsecondary interests.
In addition to admissions support (tackling immediate needs/short-term goals), PATH interventions include long-term investment in developing a stronger school college and career going culture. As part of our whole-school model, advisers plant the seeds for postsecondary success early with 9th and 10th grade classroom presentations, campus trips, whole-school activities and “culture building” initiatives.
Program Subareas:: 'Career Advising','College Advising','College Applications','Financial Aid','Financial Aid Advising/FAFSA Completion','Post Secondary Planning'
Explanation of Student Fees: Free, no cost to students

NAME: Albert D Holland School of Technology
School Name:: Albert D. Holland Highschool of Technology
Program Description::
Company One's education programming uses theatre as a means toward liberated expression, self-confidence, and cultural awareness. As actors, producers, and educators, we are committed to teaching and exposing students to theatre as a means of inspiring creative thinkers, producing well-rounded individuals and a future generation of artists. 
As part of Stage One: In-School, Company One Theatre has established teaching artist residencies within middle and high school campuses in the Boston Public School system to introduce foundational elements of performing arts, theatre production, and artistic engagement to Boston’s youth.
The curriculum embraces the BPS Theatre curriculum “Critical Learning Topics” of:
     *performing and presenting
     *creating and improvising
     *listening and viewing – encompassing           
          discriminating, analyzing, critiquing,
     *thinking, reflecting and judging
     *artistic literacy – enabling a student to use the  
          symbol system of the discipline and the 
          technology that promotes learning and  
          research
     *relationships – unifying themes of style, form 
          and context
     *context – understanding the historical, cultural, 
          and community-based context
     *value of the arts – understanding arts in one’s 
          own life and in the improvement of society

As part of Stage One: In-School, Company One Theatre has established teaching artist residencies within middle and high school campuses in the Boston Public School system to introduce foundational elements of performing arts, theatre production, and artistic engagement to Boston’s youth.
The curriculum embraces the BPS Theatre curriculum “Critical Learning Topics” of:
     *performing and presenting
     *creating and improvising
     *listening and viewing – encompassing           
          discriminating, analyzing, critiquing,
     *thinking, reflecting and judging
     *artistic literacy – enabling a student to use the  
          symbol system of the discipline and the 
          technology that promotes learning and  
          research
     *relationships – unifying themes of style, form 
          and context
     *context – understanding the historical, cultural, 
          and community-based context
     *value of the arts – understanding arts in one’s 
          own life and in the improvement of society

NAME: Russell Elementary School
Program Description:: 4th graders at Harvard-Kent and 3rd, 4th, and 5th graders at the Russell are working with Courageous educators in class to explore marine biology, environmental science, physical earth and ocean science, sustainability, engineering, aerodynamics, weather, and more. In collaboration with the Harvard-Kent science teachers and Russell classroom teachers, Courageous instructors are leading weekly hands-on, engaging lessons that build critical thinking skills and foster social-emotional growth and community building. During field trips at the start and end of the school year, students travel to Courageous sailing to learn sailing skills, investigate the animals and plants that live in Boston Harbor, and build understanding of the geographical relationships between their neighborhoods, Courageous, Boston Harbor, and the Atlantic Ocean.

NAME: Horace Mann School for the Deaf Hard of Hearing
Program Description::
BACKGROUND
Jamie Robinson founded DEAFinitely in 2015 with the goal of creating an inclusive dance crew with both hearing and Deaf students who share American Sign Language (ASL) and hip-hop dance while spreading an anti-bullying message. Jamie has over 20 years’ dance experience, including as a coach for multiple college dance teams and a high school Deaf dance team. With a Master’s Degree in Deafness Rehabilitation, Jamie has worked in the Deaf community for over 20 years, primarily to expand communication access in employment and financial education. 

DEAFINITELY CLASSES  
DEAFinitely students learn basic and intermediate hip-hop, b-boy and breakdancing in ASL. The class also merges ASL and dance together to create combinations that are performed by the students in front of an audience on at least two occasions by the end of the semester. Drumming may be used to help establish basic rhythm patterns and development of inner timing in a dance. Students will also have the opportunity to assist in translating words and concepts of a dance or song into ASL, as well as choreograph parts of a performance by combining ASL and dance in their own personal expression. All throughout the semester, students will learn about anti-bullying strategies and how to be an ally. The anti-bullying messages they share in class will be incorporated into their dance performances.

NAME: King Elementary School
Program Description:: Doc Wayne's Therapeutic Recess is a program for students that provides structure, programming, and oversight during the school day. This dedicated time gives clinicians an opportunity to build relationships with students and assess social emotional skills. Observations are then shared with school administration, so a plan can be made for students in need of additional support and a safe space can be created for all.

NAME: Haynes Early Education Center
Organization Name:: Eliot School of Fine& Applied Arts
Program Description::
CREATIVE ARTS PROGRAM DESCRIPTION
Eliot’s programs provide opportunity for students to practice their literacy and STEM knowledge and skills in real life applications while building and making. Our experienced teachers ignite students’ creativity, while guiding them through exploration of a broad range of materials and techniques. Students will have fun, be encouraged to explore their own ideas, learn to work as a team to solve artistic challenges, and experience a profound sense of accomplishment as they create objects using their hands.  

Visual Arts
Our visual arts class teaches important literacy skills including observation, visualization, and imagination. Students learn to embrace unusual materials and unfamiliar tools, that recycling scraps can be transformed into beautiful art, that it is acceptable to make mistakes, and that there is more to artistic skill than representational drawing. Projects may include book making, printmaking, murals, and shadow puppets.

Woodworking
Our woodworking class teaches basic woodworking skills using simple hand tools.  By making objects out of wood, students learn to measure, sand, saw, hammer, join, glue, work with texture, and more. They learn to use woodworking tools and terminology, and to obey safety rules. Students apply basic math concepts and deepen their problem-solving skills. They learn by experience that incremental steps, followed in a particular sequence, result in projects in which they can take pride. Projects may include making a nameplate, building a tic-tac-toe board and making a book safe. 

Comics
Our comics class teaches students about telling stories and conveying emotions. Students explore drawing concepts such as one and two point perspective, foreground and background, and point of view. Looking at comic strips, anime, manga, and other visual story telling methods, students create animations or comic books with dynamic characters.

Fiber Arts
Our fiber arts class teaches students the basics of working with fibers such as wool, cotton, thread, yarn, felt, and more.  Students learn about history, other cultures and creativity before making fiber projects themselves. Projects may include rugs, potholders, ornaments, or simple keepsakes through weaving, sewing, tying, or gluing. 


Tinkering & Invention
The tinkering & inventing class provides high quality, hands-on STEAM learning experiences to 4th-8th graders in Boston Public Schools and community-based sites using essential tools and materials. This program is an outgrowth of our woodworking program, which teaches basic woodworking skills such tool usage and safety, planning and measuring, following sequential steps and introduces design thinking and the engineering of moving part. The Tinkering & Inventing program transforms the classroom into exciting place to building on woodworking skills and explore new principles and skills involved in building circuitry and simple machines, both an increasing part everyday life. Students will be motivated to complete the steps involved in design and implementation: brainstorming, planning, building, testing, evaluating and redesigning. Projects may include robots, speakers, kinetic toys and other inventions. 

Wearable Arts
Our wearable arts class teaches students to design for the human body. Students experiment with different materials, using them as intended or inventing new techniques to achieve their goals. Projects may include making jewelry, scarves, belts, and bags. 

3-D Arts
Our 3-D arts class teaches students the process of sketching and making models to clarify their ideas and turn them into 3-dimensional sculptures. Students use a variety of materials such as air-dry clay, paper maché, construction paper and recycled materials. Projects may include masks and abstract sculpture.
Explanation of Student Fees: Payment for services go through the school.

NAME: Blackstone Elementary School
Organization Name:: Equitable School Partnerships of Boston, Inc
Program Description::
ESP intends to close the opportunity gap for public elementary school students in Boston by creating and maintaining enriching and impactful school-based partnerships. Our pilot program currently serves 350 students in kindergarten through 8th grade at the Hurley School. Our goal for the 2024-2025 school year is to add 2-3 additional public schools to our roster.  Depending on the size of the schools, we could potentially be serving 2000 students by the end of next year. We are collaborating closely with the Boston Public Schools Office of Engagement to determine which schools have the most urgent need for our services. We plan to target student populations that do not currently have access to the learning opportunities they need to thrive.  
At the Hurley School, we have tailored a collection of partnerships to the specific desires of the school community, managed the ongoing logistics and relationships involved, assisted with fundraising and grant-writing, and adjusted the programs based on feedback and changing needs.

NAME: Young Achievers Elementary School
School Name:: Young Achievers School of Science & Math
Program Description::
GSEMA Staff Led Troops provide leadership and enrichment programming directly facilitated by trained Girl Scouts of Eastern Massachusetts staff. Troops meet at partner schools during or after the school day and follow the Girl Scout Leadership Experience curriculum to build leadership, teamwork, problem solving, social emotional skills, and STEM engagement.

Trained GSEMA staff manage all troop activities, coordinate communication with families, and ensure consistent, high-quality programming aligned with school priorities. The program reduces the burden on school staff by providing structured, turnkey experiences while maintaining a supportive and inclusive environment.

Staff Led Troops are designed to meet students where they are and support equitable access. All youth who identify as girls, as well as non binary youth and transgender boys who feel Girl Scouts is the right fit, are welcome. Financial assistance is available to support participation and membership for students with financial need.
Explanation of Student Fees:
Participation requires the $70 annual Girl Scout membership fee. Financial assistance to support student participation is available.  Membership connects students to the broader Girl Scout community and provides access to programs, badges, and insurance coverage. 

Schools are welcome to support their School Based Troops by covering membership fees or providing additional funds for uniforms, badges, transportation, or troop outings as desired. Invoices, Memoranda of Understanding (MOUs), or Memoranda of Agreement (MOAs) can be provided upon request.

NAME: Mario Umana Academy
Program Description::
School Based Troops provide structured, leadership focused enrichment during or immediately after the school day. Troops are hosted at partner schools and led by school identified volunteers, which may include school staff or caregivers, who receive ongoing training, curriculum resources, and staff support from Girl Scouts of Eastern Massachusetts.

The program is grounded in the Girl Scout Leadership Experience and builds social emotional skills, confidence, teamwork, and problem solving through hands on activities in STEM, outdoor learning, entrepreneurship, community service, and civic engagement. Programming aligns with district priorities related to social emotional learning, belonging, and positive youth development.

School Based Troops are designed to reduce barriers to participation by meeting students where they are. We provide volunteer onboarding, background checks, curriculum guides, program materials, and continuous coaching to ensure quality and consistency. Our staff work in partnership with school leadership to support recruitment, communication, and retention.

We welcome all youth who identify as girls, as well as non binary youth and transgender boys who feel Girl Scouts is the right fit. We are committed to creating affirming, inclusive spaces and advancing equity in leadership opportunities for historically underrepresented youth.

Financial assistance is available to ensure equitable access to membership and participation.
Program Subareas:: 'Identity Exploration','Multidisciplinary','Post Secondary Planning','Social Emotional Learning','Social-Emotional Learning','Student Leadership Development'
Explanation of Student Fees:
Participation in a Girl Scouts School Based Troop requires a $70 annual membership fee for students. This fee connects students to the broader Girl Scout community and provides year round access to Girl Scout programming, leadership development opportunities, badge earning experiences, and insurance coverage for approved activities. Financial assistance is available to ensure equitable access for all students.

Schools are welcome to support their School Based Troops by covering membership fees or providing additional funds for uniforms, badges, transportation, or troop outings as desired. Invoices, Memoranda of Understanding (MOUs), or Memoranda of Agreement (MOAs) can be provided upon request.

NAME: Curley K-8 School
Program Description:: We are open to new partnerships! Girls Inc. delivers life-changing programs and experiences that equip girls to overcome serious barriers to grow up Strong, Smart, and Bold. Our program involves reading, hands-on activities, challenging discussion, movement, and the Girls Inc. cheer. Groups of 12 girls ages 8-11 work with 3-4 college woman mentors from local colleges and universities for 90 minutes per week during the academic year. Each week, participants will join a session where they will learn to value their whole selves, discover and develop their inherent strengths, and receive the support they need to navigate the challenges they face. We seek to build a strong female community that promotes the potential of students and allows them to recognize their own possibility, identify interests, and create lasting relationships. Participants will also participate in engaging activities that further their learning and foster positive relationships.
Explanation of Student Fees: Program is free.

Groups of 12 girls ages 8-11 work with 3-4 college woman mentors from local colleges and universities for 90 minutes per week during the academic year. Each week, participants will join a session where they will learn to value their whole selves, discover and develop their inherent strengths, and receive the support they need to navigate the challenges they face. We seek to build a strong female community that promotes the potential of students and allows them to recognize their own possibility, identify interests, and create lasting relationships. Participants will also participate in engaging activities that further their learning and foster positive relationships.
Explanation of Student Fees: Program is free.

NAME: Dever Elementary School
Organization Name:: Girls on the Run Greater Boston
Program Description::
Girls on the Run’s research-based curriculum leads to positive outcomes for girls, and its success has been demonstrated through a large-scale independent study conducted by Dr. Maureen Weiss, Ph.D., University of Minnesota. Findings using a matched comparison group, a longitudinal design, and quantitative and qualitative methods provide strong evidence that Girls on the Run is effective in promoting positive youth development,
Dr. Weiss’ research found that GOTR outcomes were improvements in competence, confidence, connection, character, caring, physical activity and life skills that include managing emotions, resolving conflict, helping others and making intentional decisions.
 
The study also found that girls who were the least active at the start of the season increased their physical activity level by 40% from pre- to post-season and maintained this increased level beyond season’s end.  Furthermore, girls who were more sedentary at the start of Girls on the Run reduced time spent watching TV and playing video and computer games by 20% from pre- to post-season.
 
Locally, we find the benefits of Girls on the Run are equally powerful. Overall, girls have a more physically active lifestyle, increase healthy behaviors, decrease the amount of unhealthy behaviors, and improve mental outcomes.
Explanation of Student Fees: The standard program fee is $225. However, caregivers can choose the amount they can afford based on a sliding scale, all the way down to $35. If that is still a hardship, we provide the program at no cost.

NAME: Perkins Elementary School
Program Description::
Our philosophy that people learn best by doing has remained the cornerstone of Hale’s education programs since the beginning. Whether crossing the Poison Peanut Butter Pit, learning to canoe for the first time, or searching the forest floor for salamanders, participants in a Hale education program will be having fun while engaged in lasting learning experiences.

Our education programs can be designed to accommodate groups of any size and age. Groups who have come to our challenge course have ranged in size from eight to 270 participants. Regardless of the size of your group, all of our programs are individually crafted to suit your needs.

We can send facilitators to you and run programs at your site. Experiential learning is powerful no matter where it happens, and we have experience delivering teambuilding programs at schools, in parks, and beyond.

We can design and deliver a virtual program for your students. We’ve trained a small group of our best facilitators to craft engaging online sessions, and a number of schools recently took advantage of this option to enhance distance learning.

We can send facilitators to you and run programs at your site. Experiential learning is powerful no matter where it happens, and we have experience delivering teambuilding programs at schools, in parks, and beyond.

We can design and deliver a virtual program for your students. We’ve trained a small group of our best facilitators to craft engaging online sessions, and a number of schools recently took advantage of this option to enhance distance learning.

IAH is an innovative opportunity for students to utilize the Hale wilderness and Boston Green spaces as classrooms. Through a combination of leadership development, nature immersion, student-centered learning, and a supportive, intentional community, IAH provides students the chance to grow academically, emotionally, physically, and socially. 

IAH creates custom partnerships to help schools transform their communities through experiential learning, leadership development, community building, and professional development.

Program lengths range from three days to four months, with varying frequencies (daily, weekly, several times a week, or monthly) based on goals and logistical needs.

Collaboration models are tailored to each school, with the IAH team either co-planning and co-teaching alongside school staff or leading the design and implementation process, freeing staff to engage with students without facilitating.

The school “campus” at IAH primarily consists of two cabins with four classrooms and 1,137 acres to explore. The buildings serve as home base and students are encouraged to explore the surrounding woods, trails, and ponds.  Class activities revolve around our outdoor setting. Students learn the technical and relational skills to be outdoor leaders, and are able to apply those lessons in other settings.
Program Areas:: 'Academic Support & Enrichment','Physical Health & Wellness','Social Emotional & Behavioral Health'

NAME: Boston Latin School
Program Description::
The semester long MEDscience curriculum provides: 

Each participating Boston Public School teacher:
  • Receives a semester HMS MEDscience Curriculum "menu" - with links to supplemental resources and asynchronous activities
  • Can reserve a class time once a week for  up to 12 virtual classes 

 • Is provided with continuous individual support throughout the semester. 
 
Each participating student: 
  • Experiences the  medical care environment in a telemedicine platform
  • Experiences the real-life excitement of delivering emergency room care as a trauma team member 
  • Receives asynchronous  training in medical skills, such as taking vital signs, suturing wounds, etc. depending upon access to materials.
  • Receives biology, anatomy and physiology instruction with the BPS standards aligned curriculum
  • Improves critical thinking and teamwork skills while solving realistic medical problems
  • Meets a variety of professionals/ role models and hears about  career pathways in a variety of medical fields
  • Receive a comprehensive career exploration pamphlet of opportunities in the area for volunteering and internships  
  • Receive a certification of completion of the course signed by the HMS Dean of  Diversity at HMS and the MEDscience Executive Director
Program Subareas:: 'Career & Life Readiness','Career Advising','Job Shadowing','Primary Healthcare','Science','Work-based Learning','Workplace College'
Explanation of Student Fees: There are no student fees.

NAME: East Boston High School
Program Description::
The semester long MEDscience curriculum provides: 

Each participating Boston Public School teacher:
  • Receives a semester HMS MEDscience Curriculum "menu" - with links to supplemental resources and asynchronous activities
  • Can reserve a class time once a week for  up to 12 virtual classes 

 • Is provided with continuous individual support throughout the semester. 
 
Each participating student: 
  • Experiences the  medical care environment in a telemedicine platform
  • Experiences the real-life excitement of delivering emergency room care as a trauma team member 
  • Receives asynchronous  training in medical skills, such as taking vital signs, suturing wounds, etc. depending upon access to materials.
  • Receives biology, anatomy and physiology instruction with the BPS standards aligned curriculum
  • Improves critical thinking and teamwork skills while solving realistic medical problems
  • Meets a variety of professionals/ role models and hears about  career pathways in a variety of medical fields
  • Receive a comprehensive career exploration pamphlet of opportunities in the area for volunteering and internships  
  • Receive a certification of completion of the course signed by the HMS Dean of  Diversity at HMS and the MEDscience Executive Director

NAME: Boston International High School & Newcomers Academy
Program Description:: ILA offers a multifaceted, four-step, semester-to-year-long process for migrant youth-led story creation that includes: 1. Viewing the I Learn America Documentary; 2. Writing Personal Narratives; 3. Producing Stories; and 4. Activating Stories . Our intention is to build a long-term and sustainable relationship with young people, schools and community partners.  We build on our first round of cooperation and repeat the initiative on an annual basis by using the student produced work to engage the next round of incoming students.  Through poetry and performance, photo-making, video-making, audio-making, and graphic art; youth collaborate with ILA’s culturally responsive, artist-educators to imagine new ways to express their experiences with migration. These production workshops in-turn serve to incubate “home grown” youth-led events (poetry slams, speeches, pop-up street art, interactive photo exhibitions…) meant to deepen public explorations and understanding of who they/we are and challenge the very idea of what we think it means to be “American.”  Working in the public sphere, these cultural interventions interrupt, challenge and transform the narratives around immigration. ILA’s story making/sharing/activating process strengthens connections between the youth, their peers, families and communities, as they break through social/cultural barriers and defy assumptions within and outside of classrooms.

NAME: Edison Elementary School
Program Description::
The Idori Afterschool Program is a developmentally appropriate environmental education initiative designed for children in PreK through 2nd grade. The program uses original children’s books and eco-themed characters to introduce foundational environmental concepts such as forests, oceans, biodiversity, and resource conservation in ways that are engaging, hopeful, and emotionally resonant.

Each session centers around an interactive story featuring one of Idori’s nature-inspired characters. Through guided read-alouds, open discussion, movement, reflection, and creative activities, children explore environmental topics at a level they can understand. Rather than focusing on environmental fear or crisis, the program emphasizes connection, gratitude, and the idea that small daily actions matter.

Students participate in hands-on activities that translate learning into behavior, such as reducing waste, conserving water, caring for plants, and noticing nature in their surroundings. Take-home materials and reflection prompts help extend learning into the home, encouraging families to reinforce sustainable habits together.

The program is designed to integrate seamlessly into afterschool settings, libraries, and community programs. It requires minimal classroom resources and provides educators with structured, easy-to-implement content. By combining storytelling, emotional engagement, and actionable habits, the Idori Afterschool Program plants the seeds for lifelong environment
